description: The provided image is extremely low-resolution and visually unreadable, appearing as a generic circular icon with a red central shape rather than a discernible scientific figure. Consequently, no sub-panels, axes, curves, spectra, microstructural features, labels, or scale bars can be directly observed from the pixels. Based on the paper context concerning tensile deformation behavior of low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels, where x corresponds to 0.5, 0.8, and 1.2 wt% carbon, the figure likely presents data related to mechanical properties or microstructural characterization of the three alloy variants. The processing history common to all samples includes forging at 1200°C, hot rolling with 90% reduction, intermediate annealing at 1000°C for 1 h, cold rolling to 1 mm, and final annealing at 1000°C followed by water quenching. The paper reports yield strengths of 614.9 MPa, 711.2 MPa, and 735.1 MPa, ultimate tensile strengths of 856.7 MPa, 979.1 MPa, and 946.7 MPa, and total elongations of 32.8%, 38.4%, and 56.2% for the 0.5C, 0.8C, and 1.2C steels, respectively. Crussard–Jaoul analysis indicates two-stage strain hardening in the 0.5C and 0.8C steels and three-stage strain hardening in the 1.2C steel. No caption or citation sentences are available, so specific figure-level conclusions cannot be visually verified.

This figure presents optical micrographs showing the as-quenched microstructural evolution in low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels with varying carbon contents. Subfigure (a) displays the 0.5C steel, revealing a banded duplex ferritic–austenitic microstructure with elongated phases aligned along the rolling direction; caption states the austenite volume fraction is 59.2%. Subfigure (b) shows the 0.8C steel, which retains a duplex ferritic–austenitic structure but exhibits finer and more equiaxed morphology with reduced banding compared to (a); caption states its austenite volume fraction is 84.8% and that ordered phases and κ-carbides are present. Subfigure (c) depicts the 1.2C steel, exhibiting a fully austenitic matrix with equiaxed grains and visible annealing twins, with κ-carbides uniformly distributed as reported. All specimens were subjected to identical thermomechanical processing including forging at 1200°C, homogenization, hot rolling with 90% reduction, intermediate annealing at 1000°C, cold rolling to 1 mm, and final annealing at 1000°C followed by water quenching. The scale bar in each image represents 40 μm. The microstructural transition from duplex to fully austenitic matrix with increasing carbon content, accompanied by increasing austenite stability and volume fraction, supports the correlation between carbon content, phase constitution, and subsequent tensile deformation behavior analyzed via the Crussard–Jaoul method.

Figure 2 presents TEM bright-field and dark-field micrographs with corresponding SAED patterns for Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels after annealing at 1000 °C and water quenching. Subfigures (a) and (b) show the austenite (γ) and ferrite (α) phases in 0.5C steel, respectively; their SAED patterns indicate no precipitates. Subfigures (c)–(e) depict 0.8C steel: (c) γ phase with κ-carbide reflections, where caption states the phase is (Fe,Mn)3AlCx; (d) twins in γ; and (e) a dark-field image of α whose indexed SAED pattern confirms ordered B2 domains with fine DO3 particles. Subfigure (f) is a dark-field image of γ in 1.2C steel, and caption states this contains κ carbides in the austenite matrix. The indexed SAED insets establish crystal structures and orientations for each region. Together, the images show that increasing carbon content stabilizes austenite and promotes ordered κ-carbide precipitation in austenite, while 0.8C steel additionally develops ordered B2/DO3 phases in ferrite, providing the structural basis for the differing strain-hardening behaviors.

This figure displays the room-temperature engineering stress–strain curves for low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels containing 0.5 wt% C (black curve), 0.8 wt% C (red curve), and 1.2 wt% C (green curve). The horizontal axis represents engineering strain and the vertical axis gives engineering stress in MPa. Visually, the 0.5C steel exhibits the lowest flow stress and fractures at the smallest strain. The 0.8C curve rises to the highest peak stress before necking, while the 1.2C curve sustains strain to the largest elongation with a slightly lower peak stress. The caption reports that the 0.8C steel reaches the highest ultimate tensile strength of about 979 MPa, whereas the 1.2C steel attains the greatest total elongation of 56.2% and a UTS × total elongation product of 53.2 GPa%, nearly double that of the 0.5C steel. These curves collectively demonstrate that increasing carbon content enhances both yield strength and ductility, with peak strength at the intermediate 0.8% C composition and the optimal strength–ductility balance achieved in the 1.2C alloy.

Figure 4 plots the strain hardening rate (dσ/dε) against true strain for three tensile-tested Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC low-density steels, distinguished as 0.5C (black), 0.8C (red), and 1.2C (green). The 0.5C and 0.8C curves decrease monotonically from ~3500 MPa toward failure near strains of ~0.28 and ~0.34, respectively. The 1.2C curve drops sharply at first but then stabilizes into a prolonged plateau with a secondary rise, sustaining rates above ~1500 MPa up to ~0.44 strain. As the caption and cited sentences state, these shapes correspond to different multi-stage hardening regimes: two-stage for 0.5C and 0.8C steels, and three-stage for 1.2C steel. The extended hardening of 1.2C explains its superior ductility (56.2% elongation), while 0.8C achieves a better strength–ductility balance (979.1 MPa UTS, 38.4% elongation) than 0.5C (856.7 MPa, 32.8%), highlighting how carbon content and attendant κ-carbide/ordered-phase evolution govern deformation mechanisms.

The figure presents Crussard–Jaoul (C–J) analysis curves plotting ln(dσ/dε) versus ln(ε) for low-density Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels: (a) 0.5C steel, (b) 0.8C steel, and (c) 1.2C steel. Panels (a) and (b) both display two-stage strain hardening behavior, with transitions at ε=0.025 for 0.5C steel and ε=0.022 for 0.8C steel; the latter exhibits a slightly steeper initial slope, which the authors attribute to ordered precipitates (κ-carbides and B2/DO3 domains) in the austenite and ferrite phases. In contrast, panel (c) for the fully austenitic 1.2C steel shows a distinct three-stage hardening response, with inflection points at ε=0.026 and ε=0.137, where the hardening rate plateaus and subsequently rises again at higher strains. Collectively, the curves demonstrate that increasing carbon content and associated microstructural evolution—from duplex ferrite–austenite without precipitates to austenite with ordered phases—fundamentally alter the work-hardening stages, rationalizing the superior strain-hardening capacity and large uniform elongation in 1.2C steel compared with the 0.5C and 0.8C counterparts.

Figure 6 presents TEM micrographs capturing the microstructural evolution of Fe–18Mn–10Al–0.5C steel during tensile deformation, supporting a two-stage strain-hardening mechanism revealed by Crussard–Jaoul analysis. At an early true strain of 0.02, subfigure (a) shows planar slip traces in austenite (γ), indicating multiple slip systems are activated even at low strain, while subfigure (b) reveals dislocation tangles in ferrite (α). At a higher true strain of 0.18, the deformation structure becomes more complex: subfigure (c) displays intensive dislocation slip in austenite, subfigure (d) exhibits a Taylor lattice (a low-energy dislocation configuration equivalent to tangles in wavy-slip materials), and subfigure (e) shows well-developed microbands in austenite; concurrently, subfigure (f) documents dense dislocation tangles in ferrite. These observations correlate with the first-stage planar slip and second-stage Taylor lattice/microband formation in austenite that sustain strain hardening, contrasting with the dominant single slip and ordered-phase hindrance reported for 0.8C steel.
